ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 34731007 refers to coffee as a substance; used as closest parent for brewed coffee beverage.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of freshly brewed black coffee, approximately 240 ml, as typically served at a coffee shop or café.

nutrient_source.title: Coffee, brewed from grounds, prepared with tap water (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 171890)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from USDA SR Legacy entry for brewed coffee prepared with tap water, per 100g. This represents plain black brewed coffee without milk or sugar, consistent with the base product described.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Beverages — Black Coffee (no carbohydrate content; GI effectively zero by convention)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed black coffee contains negligible carbohydrates (0g per 100g per USDA SR Legacy). The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature confirm that beverages with no glycemic carbohydrates have an effectively zero glycemic response. GI is set to 1 (lowest valid positive value by convention) and gi_category is 'low'. Glycemic load per 240ml serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g is 0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (2 mg per 100g); caffeine may cause transient blood pressure elevation
hypertension.guidance: Plain brewed black coffee is very low in sodium and generally suitable for individuals with hypertension; however, caffeine can transiently raise blood pressure, so individuals with poorly controlled hypertension should limit intake to 1–2 cups per day and avoid adding high-sodium creamers.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero glycemic carbohydrates; no impact on postprandial blood glucose when consumed plain
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain brewed black coffee contains no glycemic carbohydrates and does not raise blood glucose; epidemiological evidence suggests moderate unsweetened coffee consumption may be associated with reduced risk of Type 2 Diabetes, but individuals should avoid adding sugar or high-calorie creamers.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and cholesterol; contains antioxidant polyphenols (chlorogenic acids)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain brewed black coffee is virtually free of saturated fat and cholesterol, and its polyphenol content may confer modest cardioprotective antioxidant effects; moderate consumption of 3–4 cups per day has been associated with neutral to beneficial cardiovascular outcomes in prospective cohort studies.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Freshly Brewed Coffee is a hot beverage prepared by passing hot water through ground roasted coffee beans, typically using drip, filter, or pour-over methods. The resulting liquid is a dark, aromatic drink with a slightly bitter, complex flavour profile and negligible caloric content when consumed plain (approximately 1 kcal per 100g). It contains no glycemic carbohydrates, virtually no fat, and only trace protein, making it one of the lowest-calorie beverages available. Nutritionally, brewed black coffee is notable for its potassium content (~49 mg per 100g), B-vitamins including riboflavin and niacin, and a rich array of antioxidant polyphenols, particularly chlorogenic acids. Caffeine content is approximately 40 mg per 100g (roughly 95 mg per standard 240 ml cup). In Singapore, freshly brewed coffee is widely available at cafés such as Starbucks and is endorsed by the Health Promotion Board as a no-added-sugar beverage option. Clinically, plain black coffee has a negligible glycemic index and glycemic load, making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease, provided it is consumed without added sugar, cream, or high-calorie syrups. The beverage is naturally vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in brewed coffee?
Is black coffee good for diabetes?
What is the GI of coffee?
How much caffeine in a cup of brewed coffee?
Is black coffee suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171890), an approved primary source for generic Western beverages. GI is set by convention to 1 (effectively zero) consistent with University of Sydney GI Database methodology for zero-carbohydrate beverages. Ontology codes are verified against FoodOn and FoodEx2 published vocabularies.
notes: Plain brewed black coffee (no milk, no sugar) is the base product assessed here. The field survey listing at Starbucks Singapore may be served with optional milk and sugar; nutritional values here reflect the plain black coffee base only. energy_kcal is 1.0 kcal per 100g (naturally very low, not zero). protein_g is 0.12g (trace, naturally present from coffee extraction). fat_g is 0.02g (trace, naturally present). carbohydrate_g is 0.0g: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; naturally zero as brewed black coffee contains no glycemic carbohydrates. sugar_g is 0.0g: naturally zero for plain brewed coffee with no added sugar. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0g: naturally zero as coffee is a liquid extract. saturated_fat_g is 0.0g: confirmed zero per USDA source. trans_fat_g is 0.0g: confirmed zero per USDA source. polyunsaturated_fat_g is 0.0g: confirmed zero per USDA source. cholesterol_mg is 0.0g: confirmed zero per USDA source. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source. selenium_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source. monounsaturated_fat_g is 0.01g per USDA source. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g is 0. HPB Singapore endorsement noted in input description: 'Drinks endorsed no added sugar by Health Promotion Board.' Caffeine content (~40 mg/100g) is not captured in the per_100g schema but is clinically relevant and noted here. The Health Promotion Board endorsement applies to the plain no-added-sugar version.
